title: Polymer clay chevron cane
subtitle: Building a repeating chevron pattern inside a polymer clay log
excerpt: A millefiori-style cane technique in which two contrasting colours of polymer clay are layered into a chevron pattern. The cane is built as a log and then sliced to reveal the repeated pattern on the cut face. Slices can be applied to beads, pendants, or other clay forms. No kiln or wheel required.
sourceNotes: Polymer clay millefiori cane technique derived from first principles. Well-established in polymer clay community.

glossaryTerms.definition: A decorative technique in which patterned canes of clay or glass are sliced to reveal a repeated cross-sectional design.
A log of polymer clay built up in layers to contain a repeating pattern visible at each cut face.
Working polymer clay with the hands until it is soft, warm, and uniformly pliable.

content.text: A cane is a log of polymer clay that contains a pattern running through its length, visible each time you slice it. The
millefiori
tradition comes from Venetian glasswork. In polymer clay it is one of the core techniques for surface decoration.
When to use it
Build a chevron
cane

when you want a geometric repeating pattern across multiple pieces. Cane slices are applied to flat beads, the faces of pendants, or as a veneer on larger clay shapes. Build the cane once and use the slices across many projects.
What you need
Polymer clay, two contrasting colours, 30 g each
Roller or acrylic rod
Craft knife or fettling knife with a straight blade
Smooth, non-stick board
Step-by-step
Condition both clay colours until soft.
Conditioning
is essential before cane work: uneven softness causes distortion when the cane is reduced.
Roll each colour into a flat sheet about 3 mm thick and roughly 80 x 50 mm. Stack the two sheets on top of each other and press gently to bond.

Cut the stacked sheet in half across its width. Stack the two halves on top of each other so you now have four alternating layers. Repeat: cut in half, stack. You should end up with 8-16 layers depending on how many times you repeat.

Cut the layered block diagonally from corner to corner to produce two triangular wedges. Flip one wedge so the point faces down and press the two pieces together to form a chevron. The colour layers now form a V-shape when viewed from the end.

To use the cane, allow it to cool and firm up for 10-15 minutes (or briefly chill in the fridge). Slice cleanly with a straight blade: a rocking motion causes distortion. Each slice reveals the chevron pattern.
What it looks like when it is working
Each slice shows a clean chevron of alternating colour stripes. The stripes are even in width and the V-shape is centred. The cut face shows no smearing between colours.
Common mistakes
Colours bleed into each other: caused by over-warm clay. Chill the cane before slicing.
Chevron is offset rather than centred: the two wedges were not aligned precisely when pressed together. Check alignment before pressing.
Slices distort: blade was not clean and straight. Use a fresh craft knife blade and press straight down.
